prompt: Which Obsidian plugins can effectively replicate Notion's multi-view database functionality (including Table, Kanban, Calendar, and List views)? Please provide a detailed comparison of the strengths and weaknesses of these plugins.
article:
# Obsidian as a replacement for Notion-style multi-view databases, 2025–2026

## Executive answer

If the requirement is **credible replacement of Notion-style multi-view databases inside Obsidian**, the answer is now split across **three genuinely viable architectures**, not one:

1. **Best strategic long-term choice:** **Bases + view plugins**.  
   This is the strongest default recommendation because Bases is now a **core** Obsidian capability, launched in **August 2025** and expanded in **November 2025** with **List view, Map view, Group results, and the Bases API**; it stores its source of truth in local Markdown files and properties, and it already supports **multiple views per base**. The remaining Notion-like gaps are specifically visible on Obsidian’s own roadmap: **Calendar view for Bases** and **Kanban view for Bases** were still **Planned**, not shipped, at the researched snapshot, so the best current implementation is a **Bases-centered stack** with community view plugins rather than Bases alone [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[2]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views) [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/) [[4]](https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view)(https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view) [[143]](https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases)(https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases) [[144]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases) [[153]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-kanban-plugin/109179)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-kanban-plugin/109179) [[155]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507) [[168]](https://github.com/mderazon/obsidian-base-board)(https://github.com/mderazon/obsidian-base-board).

2. **Best current all-in-one plugin:** **Make.md**.  
   In the retrieved evidence, Make.md is the strongest single-plugin attempt because it explicitly claims **list, table, board, and calendar-style views**, plus **two-way relations, formulas, and rollups** through its **Contexts** model. No other current plugin in the evidence combines all of those in one documented surface. The cost is that it achieves that breadth partly by introducing its own application layer, including `.space` files and SQLite-backed context state, which makes it more powerful but less “just Markdown + YAML” than a Bases-centered workflow [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[130]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started) [[231]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts) [[232]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts) [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views) [[234]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood).

3. **Best power-user/database-programmer approach:** **Dataview-centered stack**.  
   Dataview remains the query powerhouse. Its own docs describe it as a live metadata index and query engine that scales to **hundreds of thousands of annotated notes**, and it still had **2025 releases** despite a maintainer transition. But Dataview’s own documentation is blunt: it is for **displaying, not editing**. It natively provides exactly **four** query types—`LIST`, `TABLE`, `TASK`, and `CALENDAR`—and **does not natively provide a board/Kanban view**. So a Dataview-based replacement always becomes a stack: Dataview for querying, Metadata Menu/Meta Bind for editing, Kanban/bridge plugins for boards, and Full Calendar or task-calendar tools for richer scheduling [[39]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview) [[42]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases) [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/) [[79]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/) [[86]](https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/)(https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/) [[148]](https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/)(https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/) [[241]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater) [[267]](https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin)(https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin) [[270]](https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater)(https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater) [[100]](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar)(https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar) [[238]](https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/user/calendars/local/)(https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/user/calendars/local/) [[273]](https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar)(https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar).

The strongest **negative** finding is just as important: for new mission-critical builds, **original Projects**, **DB Folder**, **DataLoom / Notion-Like Tables**, and **Obsidian Database Plugin** should be treated as **historically important but strategically weak foundations** because the gathered evidence shows either **archival**, **explicit discontinuation**, or long-term inactivity. In practice, that means their feature lists matter less than their maintenance state [[7]](https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects) [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder) [[223]](https://github.com/trey-wallis/obsidian-notion-like-tables)(https://github.com/trey-wallis/obsidian-notion-like-tables) [[224]](https://github.com/decaf-dev/obsidian-dataloom)(https://github.com/decaf-dev/obsidian-dataloom) [[222]](https://github.com/tomaszkiewicz/obsidian-database-plugin)(https://github.com/tomaszkiewicz/obsidian-database-plugin).

---

## What “replicating Notion” should mean in Obsidian

A useful evaluation standard has to be stricter than “can show a table.” Notion’s own model defines databases as **collections of pages** where each row is itself a page, and the same underlying dataset can have **multiple views** with distinct layouts, filters, sorts, visible properties, and grouping [[205]](https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases)(https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases). If an Obsidian solution cannot reproduce that “one dataset, many views” behavior, it is not really competing with Notion databases; it is competing with spreadsheets, queries, or task boards.

For this report, an Obsidian setup counts as a **credible Notion-style replacement** only if it can satisfy most of the following against **one underlying record set**:

- **Table, Board/Kanban, Calendar, and List views** over the same notes/properties [[205]](https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases)(https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases).  
- **Direct property editing** from the working view, not just by opening the raw note and editing YAML manually [[205]](https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases)(https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases).  
- **Schema-like properties** with typed fields and repeatable structure [[205]](https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases)(https://www.notion.com/help/intro-to-databases) [[207]](https://www.notion.com/help/database-templates)(https://www.notion.com/help/database-templates).  
- **Relations and rollups**, because in Notion these are not edge features; they are core database properties. Notion supports one-way and **two-way relations**, optional one-to-one limits, and rollups with concrete calculations such as `Count all`, `Count unique values`, `Sum`, `Average`, `Min`, `Max`, `Earliest date`, and `Latest date` [[206]](https://www.notion.com/help/relations-and-rollups)(https://www.notion.com/help/relations-and-rollups).  
- **Templates** for creating new records/pages with predefined content and properties [[207]](https://www.notion.com/help/database-templates)(https://www.notion.com/help/database-templates).  
- **Inline editing ergonomics** close enough to actual use, not just display.  
- **Mobile usability**, including touch behavior and absence of heavy dependence on desktop-only interactions [[208]](https://obsidian.md/help/mobile)(https://obsidian.md/help/mobile) [[209]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/getting-my-plugin-working-on-mobile-devices/29816)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/getting-my-plugin-working-on-mobile-devices/29816).  
- **Offline durability**, meaning the notes remain the source of truth locally rather than depending on a cloud database [[212]](https://obsidian.md/help/import)(https://obsidian.md/help/import) [[213]](https://www.notion.com/help/guides/working-offline-in-notion-everything-you-need-to-know)(https://www.notion.com/help/guides/working-offline-in-notion-everything-you-need-to-know).  
- **Sync tolerance**, so multi-device use does not break because some critical state lives only in per-device plugin settings [[210]](https://help.obsidian.md/sync/settings)(https://help.obsidian.md/sync/settings) [[211]](https://obsidian.md/help/sync/troubleshoot)(https://obsidian.md/help/sync/troubleshoot).  
- **Ease of setup**, because a solution that technically works only after six companion plugins and custom DataviewJS is not equivalent to a turnkey multi-view database for ordinary users [[215]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/obsidian-sync-how-to-sync-only-some-community-plugins-to-mobile/62804)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/obsidian-sync-how-to-sync-only-some-community-plugins-to-mobile/62804) [[216]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/use-obsidian-sync-and-obsidian-mobile-folder/65602)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/use-obsidian-sync-and-obsidian-mobile-folder/65602).  
- **Future-proofing**, meaning the data should still be readable and migratable if a plugin is abandoned [[212]](https://obsidian.md/help/import)(https://obsidian.md/help/import) [[219]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/what-are-the-limits-for-plugins-and-snippets-to-keep-notes-safe-for-long-term-on-the-vault/66988)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/what-are-the-limits-for-plugins-and-snippets-to-keep-notes-safe-for-long-term-on-the-vault/66988) [[262]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/it-is-hoped-that-obsidian-will-not-be-bound-by-the-framework-of-md/10480)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/it-is-hoped-that-obsidian-will-not-be-bound-by-the-framework-of-md/10480) [[263]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/open-sourcing-of-obsidian/1515)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/open-sourcing-of-obsidian/1515).

That standard leads to an important conclusion: in Obsidian, **“replicate Notion” should mean either a single plugin or a plugin stack**, not only one plugin. Obsidian is inherently modular, and the official product direction now explicitly allows community plugins to add new **Bases view types** via the API, so a credible answer can be “core Bases + add-on views” just as reasonably as “one monolithic third-party plugin” [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[4]](https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view)(https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view) [[217]](https://obsidian.md/help/settings)(https://obsidian.md/help/settings).

A second conclusion follows from Notion’s own feature model: **relations/rollups are the line between “database-like” and “database-substitute.”** Many Obsidian solutions can show a table. Far fewer can express linked records between datasets and compute across them. That is why some plugins feel very close to Notion in day-to-day project tracking but still fall short in strict database parity.

---

## Candidate landscape: what belongs in the serious set, and what does not

The biggest landscape shift between earlier Obsidian database discussions and the 2025–2026 state is straightforward: **the problem is no longer “how do plugins fill a vacuum?”** It is now **“how do plugins compete with or extend Bases?”** Obsidian itself launched Bases in **August 2025** in version **1.9**, then added **List view, Map view, Group results, and the Bases API** in **1.10** in **November 2025** [[2]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views) [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/). That makes any pre-Bases plugin architecture a legacy architecture by default unless it offers something Bases still lacks.

### Included as current serious contenders

**1. Bases-centered stacks**  
Included because Bases is now the native baseline: it is core, stores data in Markdown/properties, supports several views per base, and is explicitly extensible through the Bases API [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[2]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views) [[4]](https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view)(https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view). Any serious 2026 recommendation has to assess it first.

**2. Make.md**  
Included because it is the strongest documented **single-plugin** attempt to provide multi-view databases plus higher-order schema features such as relations, formulas, rollups, and multiple view types [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[231]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts) [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views).

**3. Projects Plus**  
Included because it is one of the few current plugins explicitly built around the classic Notion-like workflow of choosing a source set and switching among **table, board, calendar, and gallery** while editing note fields directly in the interface [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md) [[165]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/projects-plus-plugin/106826)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/projects-plus-plugin/106826).

**4. Dataview-centered stacks**  
Included because Dataview remains a central engine for metadata querying, still had **2025 releases**—including **0.5.68** and **0.5.70 (Beta)** in **March and April 2025**—and its docs still claim performance at the scale of **hundreds of thousands of annotated notes** [[42]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases) [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/). It is not the easiest route, but it is still the most extensible route.

**5. Companion plugins that matter operationally**  
These are not full replacements by themselves, but they belong in the serious candidate set because they close critical feature gaps:
- **Metadata Menu** for metadata editing across frontmatter and Dataview-style inline fields [[9]](https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/)(https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/) [[148]](https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/)(https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/).  
- **Meta Bind** for interactive controls bound to properties [[267]](https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin)(https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin) [[268]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/meta-bind-within-dataviewjs/94626)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/meta-bind-within-dataviewjs/94626).  
- **Kanban** and **Kanban Status Updater** for board workflows plus metadata synchronization [[239]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/kanban-plugin/17082)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/kanban-plugin/17082) [[241]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater).  
- **Calendar Bases**, **Full Calendar**, and **Tasks Calendar** for calendar views [[143]](https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases)(https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases) [[100]](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar)(https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar) [[273]](https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar)(https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar).  
- **Templater** for note-as-record creation workflows [[270]](https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater)(https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater).

### Watchlist, not default recommendation

**Datacore** belongs here. It is described as a **work-in-progress re-imagining of Dataview**, claims **2–10x better query and rendering performance**, and promises fully interactable views, but it is still framed as an in-progress successor rather than a finished mainstream replacement [[178]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/issues/1825)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/issues/1825) [[179]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/datacore)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/datacore) [[180]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/datacore/blob/master/README.md)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/datacore/blob/master/README.md). For advanced users it is strategically important; for most users it is still a watch item, not a stable default.

### Excluded from top recommendations

**Original Projects**  
Historically one of the best feature matches, but the maintainer explicitly said in **May 2025** that he had **discontinued** the plugin, the repo is **archived and read-only as of Jul 18, 2025**, and it was removed from the community plugin list [[46]](https://github.com/marcusolsson/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects/blob/main/README.md) [[7]](https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects). That is too much maintenance risk for new mission-critical workflows.

**DB Folder**  
Important historically and still one of the clearest Notion-like designs, but the repo is **archived as of Jul 28, 2025**, read-only, and the captured repo state shows **1.4k stars**, **179 issues**, **99 tags**, and latest visible commit activity in the repo view dated **Feb 12, 2024** [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder). Those are strong signs of prior significance and weak signs of future resilience.

**DataLoom / Notion-Like Tables**  
Relevant as early attempts, but archived and no longer maintained [[223]](https://github.com/trey-wallis/obsidian-notion-like-tables)(https://github.com/trey-wallis/obsidian-notion-like-tables) [[224]](https://github.com/decaf-dev/obsidian-dataloom)(https://github.com/decaf-dev/obsidian-dataloom).

**Obsidian Database Plugin**  
Interesting historically, but the retrieved evidence shows long inactivity and much less ecosystem momentum than stronger candidates [[222]](https://github.com/tomaszkiewicz/obsidian-database-plugin)(https://github.com/tomaszkiewicz/obsidian-database-plugin).

The practical rule is simple: **feature breadth matters only after maintenance viability**. A plugin archived in 2025 is competing not only with today’s feature set, but with a fast-moving core platform that now has Bases.

---

## Why Bases changes the whole answer

Before Bases, the main question was which community plugin could simulate a database inside a Markdown vault. After Bases, the more important question is which solution is aligned with Obsidian’s **native data model and roadmap**.

### What Bases already does

Obsidian’s official help defines Bases as a **core plugin** that lets you create **database-like views** of your notes and **view, edit, sort, and filter** files and their properties [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases). The important implementation detail is that the data is not kept in a hidden proprietary database. Obsidian states that **all the data in Bases is stored in local Markdown files and their properties**, while the view definition lives in a `.base` file or can even be embedded in Markdown code blocks [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases).

That architecture is decisive for long-term use because it separates:

- **record data** → in the notes themselves  
- **view logic** → in base definitions  
- **layout extensions** → in optional plugins via the Bases API

This is much closer to a durable local-first design than many older plugin-specific database experiments.

Bases also supports **multiple views per base**, each with its own sort/filter/display configuration [[2]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views). That is the single most important Notion-like feature: one underlying note set, many interchangeable views. The shipped core view types in the researched window are:

- **Table** and **Cards** in version **1.9**  
- **List** and **Map** in version **1.10** [[2]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views) [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)

### Where Bases still falls short of strict Notion parity

The official roadmap matters more than community hype here because it clearly records which view types were **launched** and which were still **planned**. At the researched snapshot, **Calendar view for Bases** and **Kanban view for Bases** were both in **Planned**, not launched [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/). So anyone claiming “Bases already fully replaces Notion databases” is overstating the case.

Just as important, the gathered evidence does **not** show Bases currently matching Notion’s richer database semantics around:

- first-class **relations** between datasets,
- first-class **two-way relation behavior**,
- and native **rollups** comparable to Notion’s aggregation tools [[14]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-formula-cross-note-lookup-rollup/101990)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-formula-cross-note-lookup-rollup/101990) [[206]](https://www.notion.com/help/relations-and-rollups)(https://www.notion.com/help/relations-and-rollups).

That means Bases is already excellent as a **property-centric multi-view record system**, but not yet a full relational-database analogue inside Obsidian.

### Why Bases-centered stacks are still the safest path

Obsidian’s own developer docs make clear that plugins can register **completely custom Bases views** via `registerBasesView` [[4]](https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view)(https://docs.obsidian.md/plugins/guides/bases-view). This matters because it changes the extension model from “every plugin invents its own database” to “plugins can render the same Bases-backed dataset differently.”

Two concrete examples from the retrieved evidence:

- **Calendar Bases** adds an interactive calendar layout for Bases, requires **Obsidian v1.10.0 or later**, and uses a configured **start date** property plus optional **end date** property. Dragging items on the calendar can reschedule them by updating frontmatter [[143]](https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases)(https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases) [[144]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases) [[6]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/posts/2026-03-29-weekly-updates)(https://www.obsidianroundup.org/2026-03-28/).
- **Base Board** and related Bases-kanban plugins use a property such as `status` as the grouping dimension, then change that property when a card is dragged between columns [[155]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507) [[168]](https://github.com/mderazon/obsidian-base-board)(https://github.com/mderazon/obsidian-base-board) [[171]](https://github.com/ewerx/obsidian-bases-kanban)(https://github.com/ewerx/obsidian-bases-kanban).

The micro-level reason this is strategically strong is simple: **dragging a card or moving a calendar event mutates the underlying note properties**, not a separate plugin board file. That sharply reduces the mismatch between “what the view shows” and “what the note actually says.” It also means future migration cost is lower. If Obsidian ships native Calendar and Kanban for Bases later, a user with clean frontmatter should mostly be swapping views, not rebuilding data.

### The main migration constraint around Bases

The strongest practical limitation is metadata format. The retrieved evidence repeatedly notes that Bases is **frontmatter/property-centric**, whereas Dataview can also read **inline metadata** such as `key:: value` inside note bodies [[66]](https://practicalpkm.com/moving-to-obsidian-bases-from-dataview/)(https://practicalpkm.com/moving-to-obsidian-bases-from-dataview/) [[177]](https://www.outlinersoftware.com/topics/viewt/12498/0/obsidian-bases--any-use-cases)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-vs-dataview/90561) [[181]](https://www.reddit.com/r/ObsidianMD/comments/1rexn3l/replacing_dataview_with_bases/)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-inline-fields/100324) [[182]](https://robcoles.net/posts/dataview-and-inline-to-datacore-bases-and-yaml/)(https://robcoles.net/posts/dataview-and-inline-to-datacore-bases-and-yaml/). So a vault that relies heavily on inline Dataview fields may need a normalization step before Bases becomes the clean primary layer.

That is why the best advice is not “switch to Bases immediately,” but **“move durable fields into properties first, then let views change later.”**

## Dataview as a foundation: extremely powerful, structurally incomplete

Dataview still deserves a central place in this analysis because it remains one of the most important metadata systems in Obsidian. Its own repository describes it as “a data index and query language over Markdown files,” and its docs call it a live index and query engine over a personal knowledge base [[39]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview) [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/).

### What Dataview does exceptionally well

**1. It is a real metadata query engine, not just a table renderer.**  
Dataview indexes metadata from frontmatter and inline fields, then lets you query, filter, group, and compute over it [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/). This makes it much stronger than most “database-like” plugins at answering cross-vault questions.

**2. It supports both frontmatter and inline metadata.**  
This is one of its biggest continuing advantages over Bases-era workflows. If a user has `priority:: high` or `[rating:: 4]` embedded near relevant content, Dataview can index that; Bases-based workflows generally push the user toward frontmatter/properties instead [[66]](https://practicalpkm.com/moving-to-obsidian-bases-from-dataview/)(https://practicalpkm.com/moving-to-obsidian-bases-from-dataview/) [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/).

**3. Its performance claims are unusually strong.**  
The docs explicitly say it is “high performance” and can scale to **hundreds of thousands of annotated notes without issue** [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/). That is not a casual claim; it positions Dataview as an engine for large vaults, not just small dashboards.

**4. It remains actively maintained, though with caveats.**  
The repo snapshot showed commits in **April 2025**, and releases **0.5.68** and **0.5.70 (Beta)** in **March and April 2025** [[39]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview) [[42]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases). Release notes explicitly note a maintainer transition: **0.5.68** was described as the first release done by **@holroy** [[42]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases). So the stronger “Dataview is dead” narratives are too simplistic.

### What Dataview does not do, by design

The docs are explicit: **“Dataview is about displaying, not editing”** and “will always leave [notes] untouched” except for checking tasks through Dataview [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/). That is a design boundary, not a missing checkbox.

That boundary has several consequences:

- Dataview can generate sophisticated outputs but does not, by itself, behave like a working database UI.  
- A Dataview dashboard is easy to read and hard to operate unless you add another plugin.  
- This is the main reason Dataview stacks feel less like Notion even when they are more powerful under the hood.

### Its native view model is narrower than many users assume

Dataview has exactly **four** native query types:

- `LIST`
- `TABLE`
- `TASK`
- `CALENDAR` [[79]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/)

That means:
- **List support** exists, but is deliberately narrow. A `LIST` output is a bullet list of file links or group names and supports only **one additional information** expression [[79]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/).  
- **Table support** is much stronger. `TABLE` can show any number of metadata values or calculations in columns [[79]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/).  
- **Calendar support** exists natively, but it is not equivalent to a full interactive Notion database calendar. It is a query renderer, not a whole scheduling system.  
- **Board/Kanban support does not exist natively**. That is the critical omission for Notion-style parity.

This means Dataview is an excellent **query substrate** but not a full **multi-view interaction model**.

### Why Dataview stacks become glue-heavy

To become Notion-like, Dataview needs companions:

- **Metadata Menu** to edit fields in DataviewJS tables or elsewhere [[86]](https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/)(https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/) [[148]](https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/)(https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/).  
- **Meta Bind** for in-note controls bound to properties [[267]](https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin)(https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin) [[268]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/meta-bind-within-dataviewjs/94626)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/meta-bind-within-dataviewjs/94626).  
- **Kanban + Kanban Status Updater** to turn board moves into metadata changes [[239]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/kanban-plugin/17082)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/kanban-plugin/17082) [[241]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater).  
- **Full Calendar** or task/calendar plugins to get richer calendar UX [[100]](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar)(https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar) [[273]](https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar)(https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar).  
- **Templater** for record creation and repeatable note structure [[270]](https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater)(https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater).

The micro-level fragility comes from the fact that **these tools do not all share one canonical state model**. Dataview reads metadata. Metadata Menu writes metadata. Kanban often tracks board state in a board file. Full Calendar expects event-specific frontmatter. A workflow can be made coherent, but it stays coherent only if the user deliberately keeps a single source of truth.

### Maintenance-risk read on Dataview

Dataview is neither abandoned nor low-risk. The two strongest facts in opposite directions are:

- **Positive:** real 2025 releases and active maintenance transition [[42]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases).  
- **Negative:** the repo snapshot showed **632–633 open issues**, and the latest release in the captured evidence was labeled **Beta** [[39]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview) [[42]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/obsidian-dataview/releases).

For decision-making, that means Dataview is still a valid foundation for advanced users, but it is not the safest choice for someone who mainly wants a stable turnkey Notion replacement.

## Dedicated database-style plugins: how they really compare

## Make.md: the strongest single-plugin approximation

Among the dedicated plugins, Make.md is the most ambitious and the closest to a true Notion-like application layer.

### Why it is the strongest single-plugin contender

The retrieved evidence attributes to Make.md:

- **List, table, board, and calendar-like views** [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)  
- **Contexts** as a schema/organizational model [[231]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts) [[232]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts)  
- **Relation**, **Formula**, **Aggregate**, and **Object** property types [[231]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts) [[232]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts)  
- **Two-way relations, formulas, and rollups** [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[54]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/make-md)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/make-md)

That matters because it directly addresses the hardest part of Notion parity: not just multiple views, but a real schema layer with linked-record semantics.

### Calendar depth is better than most competitors

Make.md’s calendar support is not a token month grid. The docs describe:

- **month/week/day** modes,
- configurable hours,
- **drag-to-create**,
- **drag-to-reschedule**,
- resize-to-change duration,
- and repeat-event support via a repeat object [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views).

That is richer than the calendar documentation retrieved for Projects, DB Folder, or plain Dataview, and it narrows one of the typical gaps between Obsidian plugins and Notion.

### The real trade-off: power comes from extra state

Make.md is stronger than Bases or Projects Plus on schema sophistication partly because it does **more than expose frontmatter nicely**. Its “Under the Hood” docs say that **Contexts are SQLite database files**, while space/context/view configuration lives in a `.space` folder and note properties remain in frontmatter [[234]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood).

That hybrid model has a clear causal effect:

- It enables a more application-like UX.  
- It enables richer schema/view behavior than plain frontmatter alone.  
- But it also means more operational state sits outside the notes themselves.

For users who prioritize Markdown durability above everything else, that is the central reason Make.md is not the safest default.

### Mobile and sync risks are not hypothetical

The retrieved evidence includes specific incidents, not just abstract warnings:

- a forum complaint that Make.md **replaced the native mobile quick-action toolbar by default** [[236]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/solution-for-make-md-replaces-quick-action-toolbar-on-mobile-how-to-bring-it-back/62825)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/solution-for-make-md-replaces-quick-action-toolbar-on-mobile-how-to-bring-it-back/62825),  
- issue reports around **syncing Spaces between devices** [[189]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/make-md-sync-spaces-between-devices/65566)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/make-md-sync-spaces-between-devices/65566),  
- and mobile bug reports in plugin issue tracking [[135]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd/issues/487)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd/issues/487).

These are the kinds of failures that matter in actual daily use because they arise from the plugin owning more UI and workspace state than simpler metadata viewers do.

### Bottom-line read on Make.md

Make.md is the best answer if the user’s question is literally:  
**“What is the closest single plugin to Notion databases inside Obsidian right now?”**

It is not the best answer if the user’s question is:  
**“What database workflow will age best if plugins die and core Obsidian keeps absorbing features?”**

That distinction is the most important way to evaluate it.

---

## Projects Plus: strong visual database UX, lighter evidence for deep schema

Projects Plus is the most compelling current descendant of the older Projects model because it is explicitly framed around a familiar view-switching UX over note collections.

### Why it feels close to Notion for PM/database users

The core workflow is very simple:

1. choose a data source (**folder**, **tag**, or **Dataview query**),  
2. choose a view (**table**, **board**, **calendar**, or **gallery**),  
3. edit fields in the interface, with data written back to note files [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md).

That directly matches the mental model of a Notion user more than Dataview blocks do. It reduces setup friction because the user does not need to think in query syntax first.

### Why it is stronger than its current maturity level might suggest

Projects was historically one of the very few Obsidian plugins documented as having **table, board, calendar, and gallery** in one place [[227]](https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/)(https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/). Projects Plus inherits the basic advantage of that model while also making explicit mobile-friendly claims such as **swipe gestures**, **pinch-to-zoom**, and **double-tap to create notes** [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md) [[165]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/projects-plus-plugin/106826)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/projects-plus-plugin/106826). In a plugin ecosystem where many tools remain desktop-first, that is a meaningful differentiator.

### What is still missing from the evidence

The weakness is not that Projects Plus lacks core usefulness; it is that the retrieved evidence does **not** document Make.md-level schema sophistication. Specifically, it does not clearly show:

- two-way relations,
- rollups,
- a strong formula system,
- or a documented list view [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md).

That means Projects Plus looks strongest when the user’s real need is **project and content management over note files**, not when the need is **Notion-style relational database design**.

### Practical recommendation

Projects Plus is the most attractive current choice for users who want:
- a **visual PM/database interface**,  
- **board/calendar/table/gallery** as first-class views,  
- strong alignment with ordinary Markdown notes,  
- and less code than Dataview.

It is less attractive if the workflow depends on true relational database features or if the user wants the highest possible maintenance certainty.

---

## DB Folder: historically influential, now strategically obsolete

DB Folder matters because it was one of the clearest early attempts to bring a **Notion-like database based on folders** into Obsidian [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder). That positioning was direct, and for a time it was one of the most intuitive answers for users who wanted editable rows and columns over notes.

### What DB Folder actually achieved

The plugin’s forum announcement described a “Notion-style database window” where users could:

- add and edit rows,
- add and edit columns,
- choose column types,
- order rows [[111]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/database-based-on-folders/36204)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/database-based-on-folders/36204).

The changelog later shows substantial evolution:
- **formulas** in version **2.6.0**,  
- `db.dataview` and `db.rollup` in **2.8.0**,  
- and later hotfixes around formulas/rollups in **3.2.2** [[112]](https://rafaelgb.github.io/obsidian-db-folder/changelog/)(https://rafaelgb.github.io/obsidian-db-folder/changelog/) [[113]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/releases/tag/2.8.0)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md).

So DB Folder was not a toy. It had real editable-database ambition and meaningful calculated-field support.

### Why its architecture was always both useful and risky

DB Folder’s model was tightly coupled to folders and files. One especially revealing detail from early updates: a **select-type column** could move the file into a **subfolder** when the value changed [[111]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/database-based-on-folders/36204)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/database-based-on-folders/36204). That is not just a UI effect; it is a file-system side effect.

That design creates a strong causal trade-off:

- It makes metadata operational. Changing a field can reorganize the vault.  
- But it also couples database operations to file paths, which can make templates, links, sync behavior, and external expectations harder to manage.

For some users that feels powerful. For long-lived systems it can become brittle.

### Why it is no longer a prudent foundation

The decisive facts are maintenance facts:

- repo **archived Jul 28, 2025** [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder),  
- repo snapshot showing **1.4k stars**, **68 forks**, **179 issues**, **99 tags** [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder),  
- latest visible repo-view commit dated **Feb 12, 2024** [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder).

Just as important, the evidence does **not** clearly confirm that DB Folder ever achieved full multi-view parity. The gathered materials strongly support editable **table/database** behavior, but do **not** clearly document **board**, **calendar**, or **list** views comparable to the better current contenders [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder) [[114]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/1071)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/1071).

So DB Folder should be remembered as one of the most important pre-Bases experiments, not chosen as the default answer in a Bases-era evaluation.

---

## Original Projects: strong benchmark, weak current recommendation

Original Projects remains important as a benchmark because it showed that a note-based Obsidian plugin could deliver **table, board, calendar, and gallery** over one collection of notes [[227]](https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/)(https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/). Its model was clear: a project is a collection of notes, every row is a note, and frontmatter properties appear as fields in the views [[227]](https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/)(https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/).

It also had several genuinely advanced traits for its time:

- direct property editing from the table [[227]](https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/)(https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/),  
- board cards draggable between columns [[229]](https://www.workings.co/p/first-look-the-projects-plugin)(https://www.workings.co/p/first-look-the-projects-plugin),  
- support for sourcing from a folder or, if Dataview was enabled, from a Dataview query [[227]](https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/)(https://marcusolsson.dev/announcing-obsidian-projects/).

But its fate matters more than its feature list:
- maintainer said in **May 2025** he had decided to **discontinue** it [[46]](https://github.com/marcusolsson/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects/blob/main/README.md),  
- repo archived **Jul 18, 2025** [[7]](https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects),  
- removed from the community plugin list [[46]](https://github.com/marcusolsson/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects/blob/main/README.md).

There were also already known operational caveats before discontinuation: one user said they could do most of what they used to in Notion, but that **Projects didn’t support their iPad**, and characterized it as more self-contained and less integrated with the file tree [[150]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/replicate-notions-databases-with-relation-properties/52011)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/replicating-notion-databases-in-obsidian/63727).

So original Projects is best treated as:
- a proof that the model works,
- a reference point for what Projects Plus is trying to revive,
- and a warning that plugin-level database UIs can disappear quickly if they depend on a single maintainer.

## How composable stacks actually work in practice

A recurring mistake in discussions of “Notion-like databases in Obsidian” is to treat plugin stacks as if they were a single coherent database product. They are not. They are compositions of tools that often read and write **different representations of the same note data**.

That distinction matters because the more tools involved, the more likely it becomes that one view is accurate while another is stale.

## Stack 1: Bases-centered multi-view stack

**Components:**  
**Bases + Calendar Bases + Base Board / Kanban Bases View + optional Metadata Menu**

### Data flow

- **Canonical record state** lives in note properties/frontmatter.  
- **Bases** queries and renders that note set as table/list/cards [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[2]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views).  
- **Calendar Bases** reads configured date properties and writes back when items are rescheduled [[143]](https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases)(https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases) [[144]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases).  
- **Base Board** or equivalent groups by a property such as `status` and writes that property on drag-and-drop [[155]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507) [[168]](https://github.com/mderazon/obsidian-base-board)(https://github.com/mderazon/obsidian-base-board).  
- **Metadata Menu** can add stronger typed editing or field controls when needed [[148]](https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/)(https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/).

### Why this stack is structurally strong

It minimizes state duplication. The board, calendar, and table all point at the same note properties. That is the closest current thing in Obsidian to Notion’s “one database, many views” model.

### Where it remains imperfect

- The stack still depends on **newer add-on views** for calendar and kanban.  
- Relations/rollups remain weaker than Notion in the retrieved evidence.  
- Users migrating from inline Dataview metadata may need data cleanup first.

## Stack 2: Dataview-centered power-user stack

**Components:**  
**Dataview + Metadata Menu + Meta Bind + Templater + Kanban + Kanban Status Updater + Full Calendar / Tasks Calendar**

### Data flow

- **Dataview** reads frontmatter and inline metadata into query outputs [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/).  
- **Metadata Menu** writes metadata fields, including from DataviewJS-driven interfaces [[86]](https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/)(https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/) [[148]](https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/)(https://mdelobelle.github.io/metadatamenu/general/).  
- **Meta Bind** adds property-bound UI controls [[267]](https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin)(https://github.com/mProjectsCode/obsidian-meta-bind-plugin).  
- **Templater** creates the notes/records with repeatable structure [[270]](https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater)(https://github.com/SilentVoid13/Templater).  
- **Kanban** tracks cards in a board file, while **Kanban Status Updater** copies board-lane changes into note metadata [[239]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/kanban-plugin/17082)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/kanban-plugin/17082) [[241]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater).  
- **Full Calendar** or **Tasks Calendar** provides richer date-based views [[100]](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar)(https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar) [[273]](https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar)(https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar).

### Why this stack is powerful

This stack can express workflows that other options cannot:
- query logic across many notes,
- inline metadata,
- custom interactive dashboards,
- automation-heavy creation flows,
- arbitrarily computed fields.

### Why this stack is fragile

The failure modes are concrete, not theoretical:

- **Kanban alone does not update source notes by default**; that is why **Kanban Status Updater** exists [[242]](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-kanban/discussions/558)(https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-kanban/discussions/558) [[241]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/kanban-status-updater).  
- **Full Calendar** uses special event-note frontmatter and even reserved fields such as `type`, which can collide with broader metadata conventions [[100]](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar)(https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar) [[157]](https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar/discussions/396)(https://github.com/obsidian-community/obsidian-full-calendar/discussions/396) [[238]](https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/user/calendars/local/)(https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/user/calendars/local/).  
- **Editable Dataview tables require DataviewJS**, not plain DQL, so the user crosses from query writing into API-level customization [[86]](https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/)(https://obsidian.rocks/editing-dataview-tables-with-the-metadata-menu-plugin/).

In other words, this stack works best when the user is willing to behave more like a local app-builder than a template-driven end user.

## Stack 3: Editorial/calendar stack

**Components:**  
**Bases + Calendar Bases**, with filtered views by content type or channel.

This stack is strong because the content note and the planning record are the same object. A post draft can also be the calendar item, which removes duplication between planning and writing [[145]](https://practicalpkm.com/building-a-content-calendar-in-obsidian-bases/)(https://practicalpkm.com/building-a-content-calendar-in-obsidian-bases/). For solo creators, that is a real advantage over external planning tools.

If the workflow needs recurring events, dedicated event semantics, or external calendar services, **Full Calendar** becomes more attractive—but that is precisely where the stack gets less database-like and more calendar-subsystem-like [[238]](https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/user/calendars/local/)(https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/user/calendars/local/) [[197]](https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/)(https://youfoundjk.github.io/plugin-full-calendar/).

## Worked scoring example: how to make the recommendation explicit

To turn the qualitative findings into a decision tool, use a weighted 100-point rubric for a user who wants a **Notion-like system but cares most about durability**.

### Rubric
- View completeness: **25 points**
- Editing ergonomics: **15 points**
- Relations / formulas / rollups: **15 points**
- Mobile usability: **10 points**
- Setup simplicity: **10 points**
- Maintenance / future-proofing: **15 points**
- Openness of data / offline durability: **10 points**

### Example 1: Bases-centered stack

**View completeness: 20/25**  
Table and List are native; Calendar and Kanban are available via plugins rather than native core at the researched snapshot [[2]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases/views) [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/).

**Editing ergonomics: 12/15**  
Bases can view/edit/sort/filter properties; calendar and board add-ons write back to note properties, which is much stronger than read-only dashboards [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[144]](https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases)(https://www.obsidianstats.com/plugins/calendar-bases) [[155]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507).

**Relations / formulas / rollups: 6/15**  
Good property model, but the retrieved evidence does not show strong Notion-equivalent relation/rollup support [[14]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-formula-cross-note-lookup-rollup/101990)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-formula-cross-note-lookup-rollup/101990).

**Mobile usability: 8/10**  
Core foundation is safer on mobile than most community-only stacks, though third-party view plugins still introduce some risk.

**Setup simplicity: 8/10**  
Relatively low because the dataset lives in core and add-on plugins are “just views” over it.

**Maintenance / future-proofing: 15/15**  
Strongest possible score because Bases is first-party and expanding [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/).

**Openness of data / offline durability: 10/10**  
Data lives in local Markdown files and properties [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[212]](https://obsidian.md/help/import)(https://obsidian.md/help/import).

**Total: 79/100**

### Example 2: Make.md

**View completeness: 25/25**  
Documented list/table/board/calendar coverage [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views).

**Editing ergonomics: 13/15**  
Strong interactive app-like editing.

**Relations / formulas / rollups: 14/15**  
Best documented single-plugin match to Notion’s richer schema semantics [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[231]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts).

**Mobile usability: 6/10**  
Explicit mobile intent, but documented complaints and sync friction keep it below Bases [[236]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/solution-for-make-md-replaces-quick-action-toolbar-on-mobile-how-to-bring-it-back/62825)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/solution-for-make-md-replaces-quick-action-toolbar-on-mobile-how-to-bring-it-back/62825) [[189]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/make-md-sync-spaces-between-devices/65566)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/make-md-sync-spaces-between-devices/65566).

**Setup simplicity: 6/10**  
Single plugin, but it is a large and opinionated workspace layer.

**Maintenance / future-proofing: 9/15**  
Active, but not core, and more dependent on plugin-managed state.

**Openness of data / offline durability: 6/10**  
Frontmatter remains important, but `.space` files and SQLite contexts reduce transparency [[234]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood).

**Total: 79/100**

### What the worked example shows

The tie is informative. These two options reach similar totals by **different routes**:

- **Bases stack** wins on durability, openness, and strategic alignment.
- **Make.md** wins on single-plugin completeness and richer schema semantics.

So the real decision is not “which is universally best?” It is **which risk are you more willing to accept**:
- missing some native parity today but betting on core evolution,
- or getting more complete parity now but accepting heavier plugin ownership?

---

## Decision factors that matter most in real use

## View completeness

The field narrows quickly once the requirement becomes **table + board + calendar + list over one dataset**.

- **Bases + plugins** can now achieve all four, but only by combining core with add-on views because Calendar and Kanban were still planned in core at the roadmap snapshot [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/).  
- **Make.md** is the only retrieved candidate that explicitly documents all four within one plugin [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views).  
- **Projects Plus** gets very close with table/board/calendar/gallery, but the retrieved evidence does not confirm list view [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md).  
- **Dataview** natively has table/list/calendar but no board [[79]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/queries/query-types/).  
- **DB Folder** remains table-led in the evidence, not clearly multi-view complete [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder) [[114]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/1071)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/1071).

## Editing ergonomics

This is where many Obsidian “database” solutions quietly fail in practice.

- **Bases**, **Projects Plus**, and **DB Folder** all explicitly support direct property editing in the interface [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md) [[225]](https://rafaelgb.github.io/obsidian-db-folder/)(https://rafaelgb.github.io/obsidian-db-folder/).  
- **Make.md** also supports rich editing, but through a broader application layer rather than a minimal metadata viewer [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views).  
- **Dataview alone** is weak here because its design intentionally avoids metadata editing [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/).

The causal difference is straightforward: a database becomes a daily work surface only when state changes can be made where the user is already looking. Read-only query results create friction because every update becomes a context switch back into raw notes.

## Schema flexibility, linked records, formulas, rollups

- **Make.md** is the strongest documented option here because its Contexts model exposes **Relation**, **Formula**, **Aggregate**, and **Object** types and explicitly claims **two-way relations, formulas, and rollups** [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[231]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting+Started/Lists+and+Databases/Contexts) [[232]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Contexts).  
- **DB Folder** had formulas and some rollup-related capability by version history, but the product is archived and less clearly multi-view complete [[112]](https://rafaelgb.github.io/obsidian-db-folder/changelog/)(https://rafaelgb.github.io/obsidian-db-folder/changelog/) [[113]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/releases/tag/2.8.0)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md).  
- **Dataview** is conceptually powerful for calculations, but much of that power sits in queries and scripts rather than in a relational UI [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/).  
- **Bases** is currently best understood as a property-based multi-view system, not a mature relational engine in the Notion sense [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[14]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-formula-cross-note-lookup-rollup/101990)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/bases-formula-cross-note-lookup-rollup/101990).

If a user’s Notion usage is mostly “statuses, dates, owners, tags, deadlines, views,” Bases or Projects Plus may be enough. If their Notion usage depends on **database-to-database relations and rollups**, Make.md is the clearest plugin-side candidate in the current evidence.

## Mobile behavior

Mobile is a major differentiator because many Obsidian workflows collapse when they depend on hover menus, dense tables, or unsynced plugin settings.

- **Projects Plus** has the strongest explicit mobile claims in the evidence: swipe gestures, pinch-to-zoom, double-tap create note [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md) [[165]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/projects-plus-plugin/106826)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/projects-plus-plugin/106826).  
- **Bases** is structurally safer because it is core, and core mobile support improves with the app itself [[208]](https://obsidian.md/help/mobile)(https://obsidian.md/help/mobile).  
- **Make.md** explicitly targets mobile but also has concrete mobile UX incidents, which makes it higher-variance [[236]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/solution-for-make-md-replaces-quick-action-toolbar-on-mobile-how-to-bring-it-back/62825)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/solution-for-make-md-replaces-quick-action-toolbar-on-mobile-how-to-bring-it-back/62825).  
- **DB Folder** and some older plugins had mobile problems significant enough to show up repeatedly in issue threads [[117]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/577)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/577) [[257]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/27)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder/issues/27).  
- **Dataview-centered stacks** can work on mobile, but startup cost and multi-plugin complexity make them less reliable in practice [[43]](https://obsidian.rocks/dataview-vs-datacore-vs-obsidian-bases/)(https://obsidian.rocks/dataview-vs-datacore-vs-obsidian-bases/).

## Performance on large vaults

The evidence is directional rather than benchmark-driven, but still strong enough for practical conclusions:

- **Dataview** claims scaling to **hundreds of thousands of annotated notes** [[78]](https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/)(https://blacksmithgu.github.io/obsidian-dataview/).  
- **Datacore** claims **2–10x better query and rendering performance** than Dataview, but remains work-in-progress [[179]](https://github.com/blacksmithgu/datacore)(https://github.com/blacksmithgu/datacore).  
- **Bases** is repeatedly described in comparative commentary as faster and more efficient for property-centric use because it is native/core [[66]](https://practicalpkm.com/moving-to-obsidian-bases-from-dataview/)(https://practicalpkm.com/moving-to-obsidian-bases-from-dataview/).  
- **Make.md** uses its own caching/indexing layer (“superstate”), which can help performance but also increases architectural complexity [[234]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood).

For mainstream users, the safest interpretation is: **prefer Bases for common database-like use, reach for Dataview/Datacore when you specifically need more expressive querying.**

## Maintenance and upgrade risk

This is one category where the evidence supports unusually sharp conclusions.

- **Lowest risk:** Bases-centered workflows, because the core team is investing directly in Bases and expanding the API/view system [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/).  
- **Moderate risk:** Make.md and Projects Plus. Both are current, but both are community plugins, and Make.md in particular owns substantial application state.  
- **Higher but acceptable for experts:** Dataview stacks. Dataview is active, but issue load, beta releases, and the Datacore transition all raise complexity.  
- **High risk / avoid for new critical systems:** original Projects, DB Folder, DataLoom, Obsidian Database Plugin, because archival or inactivity is already established [[7]](https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects) [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder) [[223]](https://github.com/trey-wallis/obsidian-notion-like-tables)(https://github.com/trey-wallis/obsidian-notion-like-tables) [[224]](https://github.com/decaf-dev/obsidian-dataloom)(https://github.com/decaf-dev/obsidian-dataloom) [[222]](https://github.com/tomaszkiewicz/obsidian-database-plugin)(https://github.com/tomaszkiewicz/obsidian-database-plugin).

## Recommendations by use case

## If you want the closest current Notion replacement

Choose **Make.md** if your top priority is **one plugin that feels most like a real application-layer database system** inside Obsidian [[129]](https://github.com/Make-md/makemd)(https://github.com/Make-md/makemd) [[130]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started) [[233]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Lists%20and%20Databases/Context%20Views). It is the strongest single-plugin answer because it covers all four requested views in the evidence and has the best documented relation/formula/rollup story.

Choose **Projects Plus** instead if what you actually mean by “Notion-like” is **table + board + calendar over my notes with a familiar PM interface** rather than deep relational semantics [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md).

Choose **Bases + view plugins** if you care more about **future-proofing than one-plugin polish** [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases) [[3]](https://obsidian.md/roadmap/)(https://obsidian.md/roadmap/) [[145]](https://practicalpkm.com/building-a-content-calendar-in-obsidian-bases/)(https://practicalpkm.com/building-a-content-calendar-in-obsidian-bases/).

## If you are plain-text-first

Choose **Bases-centered workflows** first.  
Why: Bases keeps both the records and their properties in ordinary Markdown files, with view definitions stored separately in `.base` files or code blocks [[1]](https://obsidian.md/help/bases)(https://obsidian.md/help/bases).

Choose **Projects Plus** second.  
Why: it explicitly says the plugin **does not create its own database** and keeps data in the files [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md).

Be cautious with **Make.md** if your rule is “everything important should remain obvious in Markdown/frontmatter,” because its advanced behavior depends partly on `.space` files and SQLite contexts [[234]](https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood)(https://www.make.md/docs/Getting%20Started/Under%20the%20Hood).

Avoid **DB Folder** and **original Projects** for new critical systems because their archival state outweighs their historical strengths [[44]](https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder)(https://github.com/RafaelGB/obsidian-db-folder) [[7]](https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects)(https://github.com/obsmd-projects/obsidian-projects).

## If you are primarily a project/task manager

If your workflow is fundamentally a **task system**, start with **Tasks** and add **Tasks Calendar** or related views when you need calendar rendering [[265]](https://github.com/obsidian-tasks-group/obsidian-tasks/blob/main/README.md)(https://github.com/obsidian-tasks-group/obsidian-tasks/blob/main/README.md) [[273]](https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar)(https://github.com/aviatesk/obsidian-tasks-calendar).

If your workflow is **project records** with statuses, dates, owners, and board/calendar/table switching, choose:
- **Projects Plus**, or
- **Bases + board/calendar plugins** [[23]](https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md)(https://github.com/ParkPavel/obs-projects-plus/blob/main/README-EN.md) [[155]](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507)(https://forum.obsidian.md/t/new-plugin-base-board-a-property-driven-kanban-board-powered-by-obsidian-bases/111507) [[143]](https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases)(https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases).

If you want a more opinionated project-management environment than a general database, then specialized tools like **Agile Obsidian** are worth looking at, but they should be evaluated as PM systems rather than generic Notion database replacements [[266]](https://github.com/danrfletcher/agile-obsidian/blob/main/README.md)(https://github.com/danrfletcher/agile-obsidian/blob/main/README.md).

## If you run an editorial or content-calendar workflow

Start with **Bases + Calendar Bases**.  
This is especially strong when the note being scheduled is also the content object itself—draft, newsletter, video plan, article note—because there is no duplication between content storage and planning [[145]](https://practicalpkm.com/building-a-content-calendar-in-obsidian-bases/)(https://practicalpkm.com/building-a-content-calendar-in-obsidian-bases/) [[143]](https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases)(https://github.com/edrickleong/obsidian-calendar-bases).

Use **Full Calendar / Remastered** when you need:
- richer event semantics,
- recurrence,
- or external calendar integration,
